content:
How It All First Started
I took a Data Analysis class in university, where I learned R. It was a fascinating experience, so I continued learning Python Pandas after the semester during the summer break. When learning python pandas, I chose Korean Lottery datasets, wondering if I could find some interesting statistics.
And I moved into web development and have worked there so far. I wanted to integrate the Lottery EDA project that I did 3 years ago into a web service. This is how it all started, and I believed this service could be intriguing for many people.
How I Designed The Project
First of all, I wanted to deploy the application to my own server, becasue it was my dream to have my own home server. I had a laptop without a monitor at home. I found it had quite a nice spec as a server. I wiped the whole data there and installed a Linux server. I set up a fixed IP, SSH for secure remote access to the server.
And I needed datasets. However, I couldn’t find the official API. So I chose the crawling method which can be executed regularly every Saturday evening right after the game.
However I felt a responsibility as a developer. I thought It was not a good idea to crawl the data from scratch every time I configure and initialize server in different environments. For example, before deploying the server I needed to test multiple times on different environments like local, Docker and the Docker containers on my home server.
Therefore, I adopted a JSON file where the crawled data is stored. It is a temp file. When the server is initialized in a new environment, It first checks the database. Since it is its first time, it cannot find any data there. so It checks the JSON file, which is managed by Github commit history. Looking back at the commit history, if crawling occurred on the local dev environment, It automatically stores the data to the JSON file and adds it to the Github commit history. So the initialized server can use this without crawling the whole data from scratch.
And I wanted to have a consistent environment, so I chose Docker. So far, I needed three separate containers: backend, frontend and DB. To smoothly control starting and stopping all the containers, I used Docker Compose.
The First Release Is Out But More Things To Do…
As of now, a beta version is out, However, there are more steps left for better usablilty and robust engineering.

• We need SSR, not CSR. With CSR the data flickers, which harms UX.

• More features are needed. We have game result lists, a game simulation and statistics for selected numbers.
3. Optimization for the data query is needed. When using the simulation API, a full table search is used. This is not good. We need to improve this.
4. For impoving the system, We need metrics and logs.
5. Our first goal for the service is to achieve the 100 MAU.
Thank you.
